sections.subtitle: Current Form
sections.text: Wolverhampton are struggling, sitting at the bottom of the league table with just 1 point from 6 matches and no wins in their last 5 games. Brighton & Hove Albion, in contrast, are in decent form with 8 points and a strong showing in their last 5 matches, including a 6-0 win and no losses in their last 3 games.

sections.subtitle: Head-to-Head Record
sections.text: In the last two encounters between these teams, Brighton have the upper hand with a 2-0 win and a 2-2 draw. Brighton's ability to score in crucial moments is evident, while Wolverhampton have failed to keep a clean sheet against them.

sections.subtitle: Offensive and Defensive Stats
sections.text: Wolverhampton have scored only 4 goals this season (18th in the league) and conceded 13 (18th worst). Brighton, with 9 goals scored and conceded, show better balance and have been prolific in recent matches, often scoring late in games.

sections.subtitle: Key Trends and Statistics
sections.text: Brighton’s matches often see over 2.5 goals (6/6 in recent games), and both teams have scored in 4 of their last 5. Wolverhampton’s defensive vulnerabilities and Brighton’s tendency to be first to score in head-to-heads (7/9) suggest an advantage for the away team.

halftimeSections.subtitle: First half overview
halftimeSections.text: The first half at Molineux Stadium saw Wolverhampton take a narrow lead over Brighton & Hove Albion with a scoreline of 1-0. The goal came early through an unfortunate own goal by Brighton's goalkeeper, giving Wolves a crucial advantage. Both teams showed intent, with possession almost evenly split and each side managing a decent number of shots on target. However, Brighton appeared more threatening in terms of entries into the final third and penalty area, suggesting they had more attacking opportunities despite trailing at the break.

halftimeSections.subtitle: Wolverhampton's performance
halftimeSections.text: Wolverhampton managed to hold their own in the first half, capitalizing on a defensive error to take the lead. Their defense was relatively solid, with key contributions in clearances and interceptions, particularly from players like Ladislav Krejčí and Hugo Bueno. On the attacking front, they showed moments of promise, especially through players like Marshall Munetsi and Jhon Arias, who were active in creating chances. Their dominance in aerial duels also gave them an edge in physical battles across the pitch.

halftimeSections.subtitle: Brighton & Hove Albion's performance
halftimeSections.text: Brighton & Hove Albion will feel hard done by to be trailing at half-time, given their higher number of final third entries and penalty area incursions. They created more big chances compared to Wolves, though they failed to convert a key opportunity. Players like Brajan Gruda showed intent with dangerous attempts, while Yankuba Minteh contributed with key passes. Discipline has been a concern, with multiple yellow cards already picked up, which could impact their approach in the second half.

halftimeSections.subtitle: Attack momentum analysis
halftimeSections.text: The first half saw significant shifts in attacking momentum between the two sides. Brighton started strongly, maintaining consistent pressure in the opening phases with sustained spells of dominance in the attacking third. However, Wolves gradually grew into the game, especially around the time of their goal, where they enjoyed a prolonged period of control and dangerous attacking play. Towards the latter stages of the half, momentum swung back and forth, with Brighton pushing hard to equalize while Wolves looked to protect their lead with counter-attacking intent.

halftimeSections.subtitle: Key areas to watch in the second half
halftimeSections.text: In the second half, Brighton will likely continue to push for an equalizer, given their higher expected goals and missed opportunities in the first 45 minutes. Their ability to convert chances will be crucial, especially against a Wolves defense that has shown resilience. Wolverhampton, on the other hand, will aim to exploit counter-attacking opportunities, using their aerial strength to maintain a physical edge. Brighton's discipline, already tested with yellow cards, could become a factor if Wolves press aggressively.
